title: DIY Octagon Picnic Table
summary: A picnic table built on a center X of 2x4s with eight radiating spokes — every tabletop and seat board cut at 22.5°, every framing spoke at 30° and 45°, for a true eight-sided table.
description:
This table's whole structure is one big X, built first on a flat, level surface — the two long center 2x4s cross and pocket-screw together at the middle, and everything else in the frame radiates outward from that single joint. Getting that center X square and flat matters more than any other single step, since every spoke and every tabletop board's angle is referenced from it.

Once the X is down, the remaining framing spokes fill in around it at 22-1/2° between each "spoke" — pocket holes or metal brackets both work. Lay out every tabletop board before screwing any of them down permanently; boards vary in actual width board to board, and the plan explicitly recommends cutting the seat boards as you go rather than trusting the cut list numbers for them.

One structural tip from the source is worth following exactly: rotate the full-length seat support 90° from the full-length tabletop support, rather than stacking them in the same orientation — that cross-orientation is what gives the underside its real strength. If leg room feels tight once it's built, a documented reader modification extends the seat-support spokes 4" all the way around and moves the innermost seat board to the outside edge instead — a legitimate variant on the same base structure, not a different table.

tools.note: The center X must be built on a flat, level surface.

materials.name: 2x6, 8 ft
materials.note: Tabletop and seat boards — cut as you go, boards vary in actual width.

materials.name: Self-tapping exterior screws, 2-1/2" to 3", star drive

cutList.note: Both ends tapered at 30° off square, not parallel to each other.
One end at 30° off square, other end pointed at 45° off square.
Both ends at 30° off square, parallel to each other.
Both ends tapered in at 30° off square. Rotate 90° from the tabletop's full-length spoke for cross-strength.
One end at 30° off square, other end pointed at 45° off square.
Both ends at 22.5° off square. Measure and cut as you go.
Both ends at 30° off square, parallel to each other. Measure and cut as you go.

steps.title: Build the center X
steps.body: On a very flat, level surface, join the center spoke pieces into a big X with pocket hole screws and glue at the center joint. Metal straps or plates can substitute, but only on the underside.

steps.title: Add the remaining table framing spokes
steps.body: Carefully cut and add the remaining framing spokes at 22-1/2° between each spoke, using pocket holes or metal brackets.

steps.title: Lay out and attach the tabletop
steps.body: Lay out all the tabletop boards first and adjust as necessary so they meet in the center of the spokes. Screw down with two predrilled screws per board.
materials: Self-tapping exterior screws, 2-1/2" to 3", star drive

steps.title: Build the seat framing
steps.body: Mark all your leg locations. Start with the longest remaining support, rotated 90° from the tabletop's full-length support for added strength. Add the two supports cut straight on one end, then the remaining lower supports, the same way the tabletop framing went together.

steps.title: Attach the seat boards
steps.body: Flip the table back over and screw down the seat boards, working from the outside in this time. Measure and cut each seat board as you go rather than trusting the cut list exactly, since board widths vary.
materials: Self-tapping exterior screws, 2-1/2" to 3", star drive

steps.title: Sand and finish
steps.body: Sand the whole table and apply an exterior finish rated for fences or other outdoor lumber.

images.alt: Octagon picnic table built on an X-shaped center frame
